sections.narrative:
Before there was any human on earth, Allah announced to the angels that He would place a khalifah — a vicegerent — upon the earth. The angels questioned this, wondering why He would place there one who would cause corruption and shed blood, while they glorified and praised Him. But Allah said: "I know what you do not know."

Allah then created Adam from clay, shaping him with His own hands, and breathed into him from His spirit. He taught Adam the names of all things — knowledge that even the angels did not possess. When Allah asked the angels to name these things, they could not, and admitted they had no knowledge except what He had taught them. But when Adam was asked, he named them all. Allah then commanded the angels to prostrate before Adam, and they all did — except Iblis.

verses.context: Creation of Adam and the angels' prostration

sections.narrative:
Iblis, full of pride, refused to bow. When Allah asked him why, he said: "I am better than him. You created me from fire and created him from clay." For this arrogance, Iblis was cast out and cursed until the Day of Judgement. But Iblis made a vow — he would sit in wait on the straight path and come at mankind from every direction, leading them astray.

Allah warned Adam and his wife to dwell in Paradise and eat freely from wherever they wished, but not to approach one tree, or they would be among the wrongdoers.

verses.context: Iblis refuses and vows to lead mankind astray

sections.narrative:
But Shaytan whispered to them both. He told Adam and his wife that Allah had only forbidden the tree because eating from it would make them angels or give them eternal life. He swore to them that he was a sincere advisor.

When they ate from the tree, their shame became apparent to them and they began covering themselves with leaves from Paradise. Allah called out to them — had He not forbidden them from that tree and told them that Shaytan was an open enemy?

They replied with words that would echo through all of human history: "Our Lord, we have wronged ourselves, and if You do not forgive us and have mercy upon us, we will surely be among the losers."

Allah forgave Adam. He chose him, turned to him in mercy, and guided him. Then He sent them down to earth, telling them that from it they were created, to it they would return, and from it they would be brought out once more.

verses.context: The fall from Paradise

sections.narrative:
Prophet Nuh was sent to his people when they had begun worshipping idols named Wadd, Suwa, Yaghuth, Ya'uq, and Nasr. For 950 years he called them to Allah, day and night, in public and in private. But every time he called them, they put their fingers in their ears, covered themselves with their garments, and persisted in arrogance.

He tried everything. He reminded them that Allah would send rain from the sky in abundance, give them wealth and children, and provide gardens and rivers — if only they would turn to Him. He asked them: "What is wrong with you that you do not attribute to Allah proper grandeur, while He has created you in stages?"

verses.context: Nuh's 950-year mission

sections.narrative:
When it became clear that his people would never believe, Nuh prayed to Allah: "My Lord, do not leave upon the earth any inhabitant from among the disbelievers. Indeed, if You leave them, they will mislead Your servants and not beget except wicked disbelievers."

Allah commanded Nuh to build an ark. Every time the chiefs of his people passed by him while he was constructing it, they mocked him. He said: "If you ridicule us, then we will ridicule you just as you ridicule us. You are going to know who will get a humiliating punishment."

Then the command came. The oven overflowed with water — the sign that the flood had begun. Allah told Nuh to carry on the ark a pair of every creature and his family, except those against whom the word had already been pronounced.

verses.context: Building the ark and the great flood

sections.narrative:
The ark sailed with them through waves like mountains. Nuh called out to his son, who had separated himself: "O my son, come aboard with us and be not with the disbelievers." His son replied: "I will take refuge on a mountain to protect me from the water." Nuh cried out: "There is no protector today from the decree of Allah, except for whom He gives mercy." And the waves came between them, and his son was among the drowned.

Nuh called upon his Lord: "My Lord, indeed my son is of my family, and indeed Your promise is true." Allah replied: "O Nuh, indeed he is not of your family; indeed, his work was other than righteous." Nuh understood, and sought refuge in Allah from asking for that of which he had no knowledge.

The earth swallowed its water, the sky cleared, the ark settled on Mount Judi, and it was said: "Away with the wrongdoing people."

verses.context: The flood, his son, and the landing

sections.narrative: Even as a young man, Ibrahim's heart could not accept what his people worshipped. One night he looked at the sky and saw a star. "This is my Lord," he said, testing the idea. But when it set, he said: "I do not like those that set." Then he saw the moon rising in splendour. "This is my Lord," he said. But when it too set, he said: "Unless my Lord guides me, I will surely be among the people gone astray." Then when he saw the sun rising, he said: "This is my Lord; this is greater." But when it also set, he declared: "O my people, indeed I am free from what you associate with Allah. Indeed, I have turned my face toward He who created the heavens and the earth, inclining toward truth, and I am not of those who associate others with Allah."

verses.context: Ibrahim reasons his way to the truth

sections.narrative:
Ibrahim challenged his people directly. He asked his father and his people: "What are these statues to which you are devoted?" They replied: "We found our fathers worshipping them." Ibrahim said: "You and your fathers have been in clear error."

When his people left for a festival, Ibrahim took an axe to their idols, smashing them all — except the largest one. He hung the axe around its neck. When they returned and found their gods destroyed, they demanded to know who had done this. Someone said: "We heard a young man mention them — he is called Ibrahim." They brought him and asked: "Have you done this to our gods, O Ibrahim?" He replied: "Rather, this — the largest of them — did it. So ask them, if they should speak." They were confounded, knowing their idols could not speak.

verses.context: Destroying the idols

sections.narrative: Furious, they said: "Burn him and support your gods, if you are to act!" They built an enormous fire and threw Ibrahim into it. But Allah said: "O fire, be cool and safe for Ibrahim." The fire did not harm him. They intended a plan against him, but Allah made them the greatest losers.

verses.context: The fire made cool and safe

sections.narrative:
Years later, Ibrahim was tested with the greatest trial a father could face. He saw in a dream that he was sacrificing his son. For a prophet, dreams are revelation. He said to his son: "O my son, indeed I have seen in a dream that I am sacrificing you, so see what you think." His son — young and righteous — replied: "O my father, do as you are commanded. You will find me, if Allah wills, among the patient."

When they had both submitted to Allah's will and Ibrahim had laid his son down on his forehead ready to sacrifice, Allah called out: "O Ibrahim, you have fulfilled the vision." Allah ransomed his son with a great sacrifice. This was the clear trial, and Ibrahim had passed.

verses.context: The sacrifice

sections.narrative: Ibrahim and his son Ismail together raised the foundations of the Ka'bah in the barren valley of Makkah, praying: "Our Lord, accept this from us. Indeed You are the Hearing, the Knowing. Our Lord, and make us Muslims in submission to You and from our descendants a Muslim nation in submission to You. And show us our rites and accept our repentance. Indeed, You are the Accepting of repentance, the Merciful."

verses.context: Building the Ka'bah

sections.narrative:
Ismail was the firstborn son of Ibrahim, left as an infant with his mother Hajar in the barren, waterless valley of Makkah. When their water ran out, Hajar ran between the hills of Safa and Marwa seven times, desperately searching for water or help. Allah caused the well of Zamzam to spring forth beneath the infant's feet — a well that flows to this day.

Ismail grew up in that valley and became a prophet. He was true to his promises and commanded his family to prayer and charity. Allah was pleased with him.

verses.context: True to his promises, devoted to prayer

sections.narrative: When his father Ibrahim came to him with the vision of sacrifice, Ismail did not hesitate. "O my father, do as you are commanded. You will find me, if Allah wills, among the patient." Father and son both submitted completely to Allah's will. This act of total surrender became the foundation of the annual Eid al-Adha sacrifice, commemorated by Muslims around the world.

verses.context: Willing submission to Allah's command

sections.narrative:
Allah calls the story of Yusuf "the best of stories." It begins with a dream: the young Yusuf told his father Yaqub that he saw eleven stars, the sun, and the moon all prostrating to him. His father recognised the significance — this boy would be chosen by Allah. But he warned Yusuf not to tell his brothers, for Shaytan is a clear enemy to man.

Yusuf's brothers, consumed by jealousy that their father loved him more, plotted to get rid of him. They convinced Yaqub to let them take Yusuf out to play, then threw him into the depths of a well. They returned to their father at night, weeping crocodile tears, with Yusuf's shirt stained in false blood, claiming a wolf had eaten him. But Yaqub knew. He said: "Rather, your souls have enticed you to something. So patience is beautiful. And Allah is the one sought for help against that which you describe."

verses.context: The dream, the jealousy, the well

sections.narrative:
A caravan passing by sent their water-drawer to the well, and he pulled up Yusuf instead. They sold him as a slave in Egypt, where the minister Al-Aziz purchased him. As Yusuf grew, the minister's wife tried to seduce him. She locked the doors and said: "Come to me." He said: "I seek refuge in Allah. He is my Lord who has made my stay good. Indeed, wrongdoers will not succeed." She pursued him, tearing his shirt from behind, and they found her husband at the door. She accused Yusuf, but a witness from her own household pointed out that his shirt was torn from behind, proving his innocence.

Despite this, Yusuf was imprisoned to avoid scandal. In prison, he interpreted dreams for two fellow inmates. One would serve wine to the king, the other would be crucified. Both came to pass. Yusuf asked the one who was freed to mention his case to the king, but the man forgot — and Yusuf remained in prison for several more years.

verses.context: Slavery, temptation, and prison

sections.narrative:
Then the king of Egypt had a dream: seven fat cows eaten by seven lean ones, and seven green ears of grain and seven dry. No one could interpret it — until the freed prisoner remembered Yusuf. Yusuf interpreted the dream as seven years of plenty followed by seven years of famine, and advised the king to store grain during the good years.

Impressed, the king summoned Yusuf and offered him a position. Yusuf said: "Appoint me over the storehouses of the land. Indeed, I will be a knowing guardian." And so Allah established Yusuf in the land of Egypt with authority.

When the famine struck, Yusuf's brothers came to Egypt seeking grain, not recognising him. Yusuf recognised them but did not reveal himself. Through a series of events, he eventually revealed his identity. They said: "Are you indeed Yusuf?" He said: "I am Yusuf, and this is my brother. Allah has certainly been gracious to us."

He forgave them completely: "No blame will there be upon you today. Allah will forgive you; and He is the most merciful of the merciful." He sent his shirt back to his father — and when it was placed over Yaqub's face, his sight was restored after years of weeping had left him blind. The family was reunited in Egypt, and his parents and brothers prostrated before him — fulfilling the dream he had seen as a child.

verses.context: Rise to power, reunion with his family

sections.narrative:
Musa is the most frequently mentioned prophet in the Quran. His story begins under the shadow of Pharaoh's tyranny. Pharaoh had enslaved the Children of Israel, killing their newborn sons and sparing their daughters. Into this darkness, Musa was born.

Allah inspired his mother: "Suckle him, but when you fear for him, cast him into the river and do not fear and do not grieve. Indeed, We will return him to you and will make him one of the messengers." She placed her baby in a basket on the Nile. The basket drifted to Pharaoh's palace, where his wife picked it up and said: "He will be a comfort to me and to you. Do not kill him. Perhaps he will benefit us, or we will adopt him as a son." And so Musa was raised in the house of the very tyrant who sought to destroy his people.

verses.context: Birth and rescue from the Nile

sections.narrative:
As a young man, Musa accidentally killed a man while defending an Israelite, and fled Egypt to the land of Madyan. There he found two women waiting to water their flock, held back by the crowd. He watered their flock for them, then sat in the shade and prayed: "My Lord, indeed I am, for whatever good You would send down to me, in need."

One of the women returned, walking shyly, and said her father invited him. Her father — the prophet Shu'ayb according to many scholars — offered Musa work and one of his daughters in marriage. Musa spent years in Madyan, tending flocks in the wilderness.

Then came the night that changed everything. Traveling with his family, Musa saw a fire on the side of Mount Tur. When he approached it, a voice called out: "O Musa, indeed I am Allah, Lord of the worlds. Throw down your staff." When he threw it, it became a serpent, moving as if alive. Musa turned to flee. "O Musa, approach and do not fear. Indeed, you are of the secure." Allah then told him to put his hand into his garment — it came out white and radiant. Two signs. Then the mission: "Go to Pharaoh. Indeed, he has transgressed."

verses.context: Flight to Madyan and the burning bush

sections.narrative:
Musa asked Allah for help: "My Lord, expand for me my breast, ease for me my task, and untie the knot from my tongue that they may understand my speech. And appoint for me a minister from my family — Harun, my brother." Allah granted all of it.

Musa and Harun went to Pharaoh and delivered the message: worship Allah alone and free the Children of Israel. Pharaoh was defiant: "Who is the Lord of the worlds?" Musa showed him the signs — the staff became a serpent, his hand shone white. Pharaoh called it sorcery and summoned his magicians. But when the magicians saw Musa's staff swallow their tricks, they fell into prostration: "We believe in the Lord of the worlds, the Lord of Musa and Harun."

Pharaoh was furious. He increased the persecution. Allah sent plague after plague upon Egypt — flood, locusts, lice, frogs, blood — but each time Pharaoh promised to relent, then broke his word.

verses.context: Confronting Pharaoh and the plagues

sections.narrative:
Finally, Allah commanded Musa to lead the Children of Israel out of Egypt by night. Pharaoh pursued them with his army. When they reached the sea, the people cried out in despair — the sea before them, the army behind. Musa said: "No! Indeed, with me is my Lord; He will guide me."

Allah commanded: "Strike the sea with your staff." The sea split apart, each side like a towering mountain, with a dry path between. The Children of Israel crossed. When Pharaoh and his army followed, the sea closed upon them. As he was drowning, Pharaoh cried out: "I believe that there is no deity except that in whom the Children of Israel believe." But it was too late.

Allah said: "Now? And you had disobeyed before and were of the corrupters? So today We will save you in body that you may be to those who succeed you a sign." Pharaoh's body was preserved — a sign for all who would come after.

verses.context: The parting of the sea and Pharaoh's end

sections.narrative:
After crossing the sea, the Children of Israel came upon a people devoted to idols. They said to Musa: "Make for us a god just as they have gods." Musa was appalled: "Indeed, you are a people behaving ignorantly. What they are engaged in is destined for destruction, and worthless is what they used to do." He reminded them that Allah had favoured them above all nations.

Allah summoned Musa to Mount Sinai for forty nights to receive the Torah. Before leaving, Musa appointed his brother Harun in charge: "Take my place among my people, do right, and do not follow the way of the corrupters."

But in his absence, a man named Samiri collected gold jewellery from the people, melted it down, and fashioned a golden calf that made a lowing sound. The people began worshipping it. Harun warned them: "O my people, you are only being tested by it, and indeed your Lord is the Most Merciful, so follow me and obey my command." They said: "We will never cease being devoted to it until Musa returns to us."

verses.context: The golden calf and Harun's warning

sections.narrative:
When Musa returned and saw his people worshipping the calf, he was filled with rage and grief. He threw down the tablets of the Torah and seized his brother by the head, pulling him toward him. Harun cried: "O son of my mother, indeed the people overpowered me and were about to kill me. So let not the enemies rejoice over me and do not place me among the wrongdoing people."

Musa turned to Samiri: "And what is your case, O Samiri?" Samiri said he had seen what others did not see — he had taken a handful of dust from the trace of the angel Jibril's horse and thrown it into the molten gold. "And thus did my soul entice me," he said. Musa declared: "Then go. And indeed, it is for you in this life to say, 'No contact.' And indeed, you have an appointment in the Hereafter you will never fail to keep. And look at your god to which you remained devoted. We will surely burn it and blow it into the sea with a blast."

Musa then prayed: "My Lord, forgive me and my brother and admit us into Your mercy, for You are the most merciful of the merciful."

verses.context: Musa confronts Samiri and destroys the calf

sections.narrative:
Musa chose seventy men from his people to come to Mount Sinai to repent before Allah. When they arrived, they were seized by a violent earthquake. Musa pleaded: "My Lord, if You had willed, You could have destroyed them before and me as well. Would You destroy us for what the foolish among us have done? This is not but Your trial by which You send astray whom You will and guide whom You will. You are our Protector, so forgive us and have mercy upon us; and You are the best of forgivers."

Allah prescribed for them good in this world and in the Hereafter. He told Musa: "My punishment — I afflict with it whom I will, but My mercy encompasses all things."

verses.context: The seventy at Mount Sinai and Allah's mercy

sections.narrative:
The Children of Israel were commanded to enter the Holy Land. Musa said: "O my people, enter the Holy Land which Allah has assigned to you and do not turn back, or you will become losers." But the people were afraid of the inhabitants and refused: "O Musa, indeed within it is a people of tyrannical strength, and indeed, we will never enter it until they leave it. But if they leave it, then we will enter."

Two righteous men among them urged: "Enter upon them through the gate, for when you have entered it, you will be predominant. And upon Allah rely, if you should be believers." But the people still refused: "O Musa, indeed we will not enter it, ever, as long as they are within it; so go, you and your Lord, and fight. Indeed, we are remaining right here."

Musa, in despair, prayed: "My Lord, indeed I do not possess except myself and my brother, so part us from the defiantly disobedient people." Allah declared that the land would be forbidden to them for forty years, during which they would wander in the earth.

verses.context: Refusal to enter the Holy Land and 40 years of wandering

sections.narrative:
During their years in the wilderness, the Children of Israel constantly tested Musa's patience. They complained about the food Allah provided — manna and quails — saying: "O Musa, we can never endure one kind of food. So call upon your Lord to bring forth for us from the earth its green herbs and its cucumbers and its garlic and its lentils and its onions." Musa said: "Would you exchange what is better for what is less?"

They were also commanded not to fish on the Sabbath, but some of them devised tricks to catch fish. They were told to slaughter a cow as a sacrifice, but kept asking questions to delay and complicate a simple command until it became nearly impossible.

Despite all of this, Musa remained steadfast. He is the prophet Allah spoke to directly — Kalimullah. When Musa asked to see Allah, he was told: "You will not see Me, but look at the mountain; if it should remain in its place, then you will see Me." When Allah revealed Himself to the mountain, it crumbled to dust, and Musa fell unconscious. When he awoke, he said: "Exalted are You! I have repented to You, and I am the first of the believers."

verses.context: Complaints about food in the wilderness
Musa asks to see Allah and the mountain crumbles

sections.narrative:
Dawud was a young man when he faced Jalut (Goliath), the mighty warrior king. When the armies met, the soldiers of Talut (Saul) were afraid. But Dawud, with Allah's permission, killed Jalut. Allah gave Dawud the kingdom, wisdom, and taught him what He willed.

As both king and prophet, Dawud was given extraordinary gifts. Allah softened iron in his hands — he could shape it without fire, crafting coats of chainmail armor. He was given the Zabur (Psalms), and when he recited, the mountains and birds would echo his praise of Allah, glorifying at sunrise and sunset.

verses.context: Dawud defeats Jalut
Mountains and birds glorify with Dawud

sections.narrative:
Dawud was also tested in his judgement. Two men came to him with a dispute — one had ninety-nine ewes and the other had just one, yet the first demanded the other's single ewe. Dawud ruled in favour of the weaker party, then realised this was a test from Allah. He fell into prostration and repented. Allah forgave him and gave him nearness and a beautiful return.

Allah told him: "O Dawud, indeed We have made you a successor upon the earth, so judge between the people in truth and do not follow your own desire, as it will lead you astray from the way of Allah."

verses.context: The test of judgement

sections.narrative:
Sulayman inherited from his father Dawud — not wealth, but prophethood and wisdom. Allah gave him a kingdom unlike any before or after: he could command the wind, which would travel a month's journey in a morning and a month's journey in an evening. He understood the speech of birds and ants. Jinn worked under his command, building for him palaces, statues, basins like reservoirs, and cooking pots fixed in place.

Once, while marching with his armies of jinn, men, and birds, an ant cried out: "O ants, enter your dwellings so that Sulayman and his soldiers do not crush you unknowingly." Sulayman smiled at her words and prayed: "My Lord, enable me to be grateful for Your favour which You have bestowed upon me."

verses.context: The wind, the birds, the ant

sections.narrative: The hoopoe bird once came to Sulayman with extraordinary news: in the land of Sheba, a queen ruled a mighty people who worshipped the sun. Sulayman sent a letter inviting her to submit to Allah. The Queen of Sheba came to visit, and Sulayman had her throne brought to him in the blink of an eye by one who had knowledge of the Scripture. When she arrived and saw her own throne, she was amazed. Then she was asked to enter his palace — and seeing the glass floor, she thought it was a pool of water and uncovered her shins. Sulayman told her it was a palace paved with crystal. She declared: "My Lord, indeed I have wronged myself, and I submit with Sulayman to Allah, Lord of the worlds."

verses.context: The Queen of Sheba

sections.narrative: Sulayman's death itself was remarkable. He died while leaning on his staff, and the jinn continued working, not realising he had passed away. It was only when a small creature of the earth gnawed through his staff and his body fell that they knew. Had the jinn known the unseen, they would not have remained in the humiliating punishment of hard labour. This was a clear sign that the jinn have no knowledge of the unseen.

verses.context: Sulayman's death and the jinn's ignorance of the unseen

name: Yunus
title: Jonah — The One Who Called from Darkness

sections.narrative:
Yunus was sent to a people of a hundred thousand or more. He called them to Allah, but they rejected him. Frustrated, he left before Allah had given him permission — boarding a ship that was soon caught in a violent storm. The crew cast lots to lighten the load, and the lot fell on Yunus. He was thrown into the sea, where a great whale swallowed him whole.

In the layers of darkness — the darkness of the night, the darkness of the sea, and the darkness of the whale's belly — Yunus called out: "There is no deity except You; exalted are You. Indeed, I have been of the wrongdoers."

Allah heard his prayer. Had he not been of those who glorify Allah, he would have remained in the whale's belly until the Day of Resurrection. But Allah caused the whale to cast him onto the shore, ill and exposed. He caused a gourd plant to grow over him, giving him shade and nourishment as he recovered.

And his people? They believed. All of them. Allah removed from them the punishment of disgrace in worldly life and gave them enjoyment for a time.

verses.context: The whale, the prayer, the rescue
His prayer from the darkness

sections.narrative:
The story of Isa begins with his mother, Maryam — the most honoured woman in the Quran, with an entire surah named after her. She had devoted herself to worship in the temple, where the prophet Zakariya cared for her. Every time he entered her prayer chamber, he found provision already there. He asked: "O Maryam, from where is this?" She said: "It is from Allah. Indeed, Allah provides for whom He wills without account."

Then the angels brought her news: "O Maryam, indeed Allah gives you good tidings of a word from Him, whose name will be the Messiah, Isa, the son of Maryam — distinguished in this world and the Hereafter and among those brought near to Allah." She said: "My Lord, how will I have a child when no man has touched me?" The angel said: "Such is Allah; He creates what He wills."

verses.context: The annunciation of Isa's birth

sections.narrative:
Maryam withdrew to a remote place. The pains of childbirth drove her to the trunk of a palm tree. In her anguish, she cried: "Oh, I wish I had died before this and was in oblivion, forgotten." A voice called to her from below: "Do not grieve; your Lord has provided beneath you a stream. And shake toward you the trunk of the palm tree; it will drop upon you ripe, fresh dates. So eat and drink and be content."

She brought the baby to her people, who were shocked. "O Maryam, you have certainly done a thing unprecedented!" She pointed to the infant. They said: "How can we speak to one who is in the cradle, a child?" Then Isa spoke — a newborn, from the cradle: "Indeed, I am the servant of Allah. He has given me the Scripture and made me a prophet. And He has made me blessed wherever I am and has enjoined upon me prayer and charity as long as I remain alive."

verses.context: The miraculous birth and speaking from the cradle

sections.narrative:
Isa called the Children of Israel to worship Allah alone. He told them: "Indeed, Allah is my Lord and your Lord, so worship Him. That is the straight path." He confirmed the Torah that came before him, but made lawful some things that had been forbidden to them.

When Isa sensed disbelief from his people, he asked: "Who are my supporters for the cause of Allah?" The disciples said: "We are supporters for Allah. We have believed in Allah and testify that we are Muslims." They asked him to pray for a table spread with food to be sent down from heaven as a sign. Isa prayed, and Allah sent it down, saying it would be a festival for them — but whoever disbelieved after that would face a severe punishment.

verses.context: The disciples and the table from heaven

sections.narrative:
Isa performed miracles by Allah's permission: he healed the blind and the leper, brought the dead back to life, and fashioned a bird from clay and breathed into it so it became alive. He told his people what they ate and what they stored in their houses. All of these were signs, yet many still rejected him.

The Quran is clear that Isa was not killed, nor was he crucified — though it was made to appear so. Rather, Allah raised him up to Himself. He will return before the Day of Judgement. He is honoured in this world and the next, a word from Allah and a spirit from Him — but he is not divine. He himself said: "Indeed, Allah is my Lord and your Lord, so worship Him. That is the straight path."

verses.context: He was not crucified — raised to Allah
His miracles and his declaration of servanthood

sections.narrative:
Muhammad ﷺ was born in Makkah into the tribe of Quraysh, orphaned young — his father died before his birth, his mother when he was six, and his grandfather when he was eight. He was known as Al-Amin (the Trustworthy) and As-Sadiq (the Truthful) even before prophethood.

At the age of forty, while meditating in the Cave of Hira on the Mountain of Light, the angel Jibril appeared to him and said: "Read!" He replied: "I cannot read." The angel squeezed him and said again: "Read!" This happened three times, and then Jibril revealed the first words of the Quran: "Read in the name of your Lord who created. Created man from a clinging substance. Read, and your Lord is the most Generous — Who taught by the pen — taught man that which he knew not."

verses.context: The first revelation

sections.narrative:
For thirteen years in Makkah, Muhammad ﷺ called his people to Islam. He and his followers endured persecution, boycott, and abuse. Some were tortured, some were killed. When the persecution became unbearable, they migrated to Madinah — the Hijrah — which marks the beginning of the Islamic calendar.

In Madinah, a community was established. The Quran continued to be revealed over twenty-three years total, providing guidance for every aspect of life. Muhammad ﷺ was sent not just to the Arabs but as a mercy to all of mankind.

verses.context: A mercy to all the worlds
The seal of the prophets

sections.narrative:
After years of struggle, the Prophet ﷺ returned to Makkah in triumph. He entered the city with humility, his head bowed, and cleansed the Ka'bah of its 360 idols, reciting: "Truth has come, and falsehood has departed. Indeed, falsehood is ever bound to depart." He forgave the people of Makkah who had persecuted him for years.

The last surah to be revealed was Surah An-Nasr: "When the victory of Allah has come and the conquest, and you see the people entering into the religion of Allah in multitudes, then exalt Him with praise of your Lord and ask His forgiveness. Indeed, He is ever Accepting of repentance."

verses.context: Victory and the completion of the message

sections.narrative:
Ayyub was a prophet blessed with wealth, health, and a large family. Then he was tested — he lost everything. His wealth vanished, his children died, and his body was struck with a terrible illness that lasted for years. People abandoned him. Through all of it, Ayyub remained patient, never complaining against Allah.

Finally, he called out: "Indeed, adversity has touched me, and You are the Most Merciful of the merciful." Allah responded immediately: "Strike the ground with your foot — this is a cool wash and a drink." A spring gushed forth. Allah restored his health, returned his family to him, and gave him double of what he had lost. His story is the ultimate example of sabr — beautiful patience in the face of overwhelming loss.

verses.context: His prayer and Allah's response
His patience rewarded

sections.narrative:
The people of 'Ad were powerful and prosperous, building great monuments and towers on every elevation. They thought they were invincible. Allah sent them the prophet Hud, who said: "O my people, worship Allah; you have no deity other than Him. Will you not fear Him?"

They mocked him: "We are not going to leave our gods for your saying, and we are not believers in you." They challenged him to bring the punishment he threatened. So Allah sent upon them a furious wind for seven nights and eight days without stopping — it destroyed everything in its path. After the wind, nothing remained of the great people of 'Ad but their empty ruins.

verses.context: Hud's mission and the destruction of 'Ad
The furious wind

name: Salih
title: Sent to the People of Thamud

sections.narrative:
The people of Thamud carved magnificent homes from the mountainside — rock-cut dwellings that can still be seen in the region of Al-Hijr in modern Saudi Arabia. They were skilled and powerful, but they rejected Allah. The prophet Salih was sent to them.

As a sign, Allah brought forth a she-camel from a rock. Salih told his people: "This is the she-camel of Allah — a sign for you. So let her eat within Allah's earth and do not touch her with harm, or you will be taken by a painful punishment." He told them to share the water — one day for the camel, one day for them.

But they hamstrung the camel and killed it defiantly. Salih warned them: "Enjoy yourselves in your homes for three days. That is a promise not to be denied." On the third day, a thunderous blast seized them. They lay fallen in their homes, as if they had never prospered there.

verses.context: The she-camel and the destruction of Thamud
Their denial and the blast

sections.narrative:
Lut was the nephew of Ibrahim. He was sent to the people of Sodom, who practiced a sin unprecedented among all of creation. Lut pleaded with them: "Do you approach males among the worlds and leave what your Lord has created for you as mates? Rather, you are a transgressing people."

They threatened to expel him: "If you do not desist, O Lut, you will surely be of those evicted." When angels came to Lut in the form of handsome young men to warn him, the people of the city rushed to his door with evil intent. Lut was distressed, saying: "This is a difficult day." The angels told him: "Do not fear. We are the messengers of your Lord; they will never reach you."

Lut was told to leave with his family during the night and not to look back. At dawn, the punishment came — Allah turned the cities upside down and rained upon them stones of baked clay, marked from your Lord. And they are not from the wrongdoers very far.

verses.context: The angels' visit and the destruction of Sodom

name: Shuayb
title: Sent to the People of Madyan

sections.narrative:
The people of Madyan were merchants who cheated in their measurements. They gave less when they sold and demanded more when they bought. The prophet Shu'ayb — known for his eloquence — was sent to them.

He said: "O my people, worship Allah; you have no deity other than Him. And do not decrease from the measure and the scale. Indeed, I see you in prosperity, but I fear for you the punishment of an all-encompassing Day." They said he was just a man bewitched, and they denied him. An earthquake seized them, and they lay fallen in their homes — as if they had never prospered there.

verses.context: Shu'ayb's plea against fraud and the earthquake

name: Idris
title: Enoch — Raised to a High Station

sections.narrative: Idris is mentioned briefly in the Quran but with great honour. He was a man of truth and a prophet. Allah raised him to a high station — scholars interpret this as a physical raising to the heavens, or an elevation in rank and closeness to Allah. He is counted among the patient and the righteous.

verses.context: A man of truth, raised to a high station

sections.narrative:
When the angels visited Ibrahim on their way to destroy the people of Lut, they brought good news. Ibrahim's wife Sarah laughed in astonishment when they told her she would have a son — she was old and her husband was elderly. The angels said: "Do you wonder at the decree of Allah? May the mercy of Allah and His blessings be upon you, people of the house."

Ishaq was born as promised. Through him, the line of Israelite prophets continued — his son was Yaqub, and from Yaqub came the twelve tribes of Israel. Allah blessed both Ishaq and his descendants, among whom were those who did good and those who clearly wronged themselves.

verses.context: The glad tidings of Ishaq's birth
Blessed after the sacrifice

sections.narrative:
Yaqub's story is inseparable from his son Yusuf's. When his sons brought him Yusuf's shirt stained with false blood, claiming a wolf had eaten him, Yaqub knew they were lying. He said: "Rather, your souls have enticed you to something. So patience is beautiful. And Allah is the one sought for help against that which you describe."

Years passed. Yaqub never stopped grieving. His eyes turned white from sorrow — he wept until he went blind. His sons said: "By Allah, you will not cease remembering Yusuf until you become fatally ill or become of those who perish." He replied: "I only complain of my suffering and my grief to Allah, and I know from Allah that which you do not know."

When Yusuf finally sent his shirt back from Egypt with his brothers, Yaqub recognised the scent before it arrived. They placed it over his face, and his sight was restored. The family travelled to Egypt and were reunited. Yaqub fell into prostration, and the dream Yusuf had seen as a child — eleven stars, the sun, and the moon bowing — was fulfilled.

verses.context: Grief, patience, and hope
Reunion and sight restored

sections.narrative:
Harun was Musa's older brother, given prophethood alongside him. When Musa prayed for a helper, he asked for Harun: "And appoint for me a minister from my family — Harun, my brother. Increase through him my strength and let him share my task." Allah granted this request.

When Musa went to Mount Sinai to receive the Torah, he left Harun in charge of the people. In his absence, a man named Samiri fashioned a golden calf from the people's jewellery, and it made a sound. The people began worshipping it. Harun warned them: "O my people, you are only being tested by it, and indeed, your Lord is the Most Merciful, so follow me and obey my command." But they refused to stop until Musa returned.

When Musa came back and saw them worshipping the calf, he was furious. He grabbed his brother by the beard. Harun cried: "O son of my mother, do not seize me by my beard or by my head. Indeed, I feared that you would say, 'You caused division among the Children of Israel, and you did not observe my word.'"

verses.context: Appointed as Musa's helper
The golden calf and Harun's plea

sections.narrative:
Zakariya was an elderly priest who cared for Maryam in the temple. Every time he visited her prayer chamber, he found fresh provisions — fruits out of season, food he had not brought. He asked: "O Maryam, from where is this?" She said: "It is from Allah."

Seeing this miracle kindled a longing in Zakariya's heart. Though he was old and his wife was barren, he prayed in secret: "My Lord, indeed my bones have weakened, and my head has filled with white hair, and never have I been in my supplication to You, my Lord, unhappy. And indeed, I fear my relatives after me, and my wife has been barren, so give me from Yourself an heir."

Allah answered: "O Zakariya, indeed We give you good tidings of a boy whose name will be Yahya. We have not assigned to any before him that name." Zakariya was overcome: "My Lord, how will I have a boy when my wife has been barren and I have reached extreme old age?" Allah said: "It is easy for Me — I created you before, when you were nothing."

verses.context: His prayer answered with Yahya

name: Yahya
title: John — The First of His Name

sections.narrative:
Yahya was given a name no one had borne before him — Allah Himself named him. He was given wisdom as a child and grew up with tenderness from Allah. He was devout, dutiful to his parents, and neither arrogant nor disobedient. He confirmed the word from Allah and was a leader, chaste, and a prophet from among the righteous.

Allah's peace was upon him the day he was born, the day he died, and the day he will be raised alive.

verses.context: Given wisdom, compassion, and purity

sections.narrative: Dhul-Kifl is mentioned twice in the Quran, both times alongside other prophets. His identity is debated among scholars — some say he was Ezekiel, others that he was a righteous man who was given a portion of responsibility and fulfilled it with patience and devotion. What is clear is that Allah counted him among the patient, among the righteous, and among the best of creation.

verses.context: Among the patient and righteous
Among the best of men

sections.narrative: Ilyas was sent to his people who had begun worshipping an idol called Baal. He confronted them directly: "Will you not fear Allah? Do you call upon Baal and leave the best of creators — Allah, your Lord and the Lord of your first forefathers?" They denied him. But Ilyas is praised: "Indeed, he was of Our believing servants. And indeed, Ilyas was from among the messengers. Peace upon Ilyas."

verses.context: Calling his people away from Baal

sections.narrative: Al-Yasa is mentioned alongside the greatest prophets in the Quran — with Ibrahim, Ishaq, Yaqub, Musa, and Harun. Allah says of him and others: "All were favoured above the nations." He continued the mission of Ilyas, guiding his people to the worship of Allah alone. He is counted among the best of creation.

verses.context: Favoured above the nations
Among the best of men
